source_paper_title: Welfare Distribution in Two-sided Random Matching Markets
context:
Page 7, Section 3.1 (Discussion); reiterated page 18, Section 7.1 (after Proposition 7.3).

While we will establish such an approximation, we believe that, with a refined analysis, one should be able to show \(\sup_{\mu\in S}\,\bigl|n^{-1}\|X(\mu)\|_1\|Y(\mu)\|_1-1\bigr|\xrightarrow{p}0\).

We believe, however, that a refined analysis should suggest that \(\sup_{\mu\in S}\,\bigl|n^{-1}\|X(\mu)\|_1\|Y(\mu)\|_1-1\bigr|\xrightarrow{p}0\). This is the analogue of the "law of hyperbola" in Pittel (1992).
title: Remove truncation in hyperbola law for stable matching values in logit markets
background_latex:
Consider a two-sided matching market with 
1) men set \(M=\{m_1,\dots,m_n\}\) and women set \(W=\{w_1,\dots,w_n\}\), and a (perfect) matching \(\mu:M\to W\).
2) For each ordered pair \((m_i,w_j)\), men have latent values \(X_{ij}\sim \mathrm{Exp}(a_{ij})\) (independent across \(i,j\)), and women have latent values \(Y_{ji}\sim \mathrm{Exp}(b_{ji})\) (independent across \(j,i\)), where \(\mathrm{Exp}(\lambda)\) denotes the exponential distribution with rate \(\lambda\). Smaller latent values are better.
3) Preferences are induced by these latent values: man \(m_i\) ranks women by increasing \(X_{ij}\), and woman \(w_j\) ranks men by increasing \(Y_{ji}\).
4) A matching \(\mu\) is stable if there is no blocking pair \((m_i,w_j)\) such that \(X_{ij}<X_{i\mu(i)}\) and \(Y_{ji}<Y_{j\mu^{-1}(j)}\).
5) Let \(S\) denote the (random) set of stable matchings.
6) A contiguity/boundedness condition holds: after an appropriate positive rescaling, the scores satisfy uniform bounds \(a_{ij}, b_{ji}\in[1/C,C]\) and the mutual matrix \(M=(m_{ij})\) with \(m_{ij}=\frac{1}{n}a_{ij}b_{ji}\) is bistochastic and satisfies \(nm_{ij}\in[1/C,C]\), for some absolute constant \(C\ge 1\).
7) For a matching \(\mu\), define the realized partner values
\[X_i(\mu):=X_{i\mu(i)},\qquad Y_j(\mu):=Y_{j\mu^{-1}(j)}.\]
Let \(\|X(\mu)\|_1=\sum_{i=1}^n X_i(\mu)\) and \(\|Y(\mu)\|_1=\sum_{j=1}^n Y_j(\mu)\).
8) For \(\delta\in(0,1)\), define a \(\delta\)-truncation procedure on a stable matching \(\mu\) that removes the least satisfied \(\lfloor \delta n/2\rfloor\) men and the least satisfied \(\lfloor \delta n/2\rfloor\) women (those with the largest partner values), and considers the induced partial matching on the remaining \(n-\lfloor \delta n\rfloor\) pairs. Let \(X_\delta(\mu)\) and \(Y_\delta(\mu)\) denote the resulting (length-\(n\)) vectors with removed agents’ entries set to \(0\).
problem_definition_latex:
Show that the "hyperbola law" for total welfare holds without truncation, namely that
\[\sup_{\mu\in S}\Bigl|\frac{1}{n}\,\|X(\mu)\|_1\,\|Y(\mu)\|_1-1\Bigr|\xrightarrow{p}0\quad\text{as }n\to\infty.\]
Equivalently, remove the need for \(\delta\)-truncation in the existing bound \(\frac{1}{n}\|X_\delta(\mu)\|_1\|Y_\delta(\mu)\|_1\approx 1\) that holds uniformly over stable matchings, and prove the corresponding statement for the full sums \(\|X(\mu)\|_1\) and \(\|Y(\mu)\|_1\).
latex:
Consider a two-sided matching market with 
1) men set \(M=\{m_1,\dots,m_n\}\) and women set \(W=\{w_1,\dots,w_n\}\), and a (perfect) matching \(\mu:M\to W\).
2) For each ordered pair \((m_i,w_j)\), men have latent values \(X_{ij}\sim \mathrm{Exp}(a_{ij})\) (independent across \(i,j\)), and women have latent values \(Y_{ji}\sim \mathrm{Exp}(b_{ji})\) (independent across \(j,i\)), where \(\mathrm{Exp}(\lambda)\) denotes the exponential distribution with rate \(\lambda\). Smaller latent values are better.
3) Preferences are induced by these latent values: man \(m_i\) ranks women by increasing \(X_{ij}\), and woman \(w_j\) ranks men by increasing \(Y_{ji}\).
4) A matching \(\mu\) is stable if there is no blocking pair \((m_i,w_j)\) such that \(X_{ij}<X_{i\mu(i)}\) and \(Y_{ji}<Y_{j\mu^{-1}(j)}\).
5) Let \(S\) denote the (random) set of stable matchings.
6) A contiguity/boundedness condition holds: after an appropriate positive rescaling, the scores satisfy uniform bounds \(a_{ij}, b_{ji}\in[1/C,C]\) and the mutual matrix \(M=(m_{ij})\) with \(m_{ij}=\frac{1}{n}a_{ij}b_{ji}\) is bistochastic and satisfies \(nm_{ij}\in[1/C,C]\), for some absolute constant \(C\ge 1\).
7) For a matching \(\mu\), define the realized partner values
\[X_i(\mu):=X_{i\mu(i)},\qquad Y_j(\mu):=Y_{j\mu^{-1}(j)}.\]
Let \(\|X(\mu)\|_1=\sum_{i=1}^n X_i(\mu)\) and \(\|Y(\mu)\|_1=\sum_{j=1}^n Y_j(\mu)\).
8) For \(\delta\in(0,1)\), define a \(\delta\)-truncation procedure on a stable matching \(\mu\) that removes the least satisfied \(\lfloor \delta n/2\rfloor\) men and the least satisfied \(\lfloor \delta n/2\rfloor\) women (those with the largest partner values), and considers the induced partial matching on the remaining \(n-\lfloor \delta n\rfloor\) pairs. Let \(X_\delta(\mu)\) and \(Y_\delta(\mu)\) denote the resulting (length-\(n\)) vectors with removed agents’ entries set to \(0\).

Show that the "hyperbola law" for total welfare holds without truncation, namely that
\[\sup_{\mu\in S}\Bigl|\frac{1}{n}\,\|X(\mu)\|_1\,\|Y(\mu)\|_1-1\Bigr|\xrightarrow{p}0\quad\text{as }n\to\infty.\]
Equivalently, remove the need for \(\delta\)-truncation in the existing bound \(\frac{1}{n}\|X_\delta(\mu)\|_1\|Y_\delta(\mu)\|_1\approx 1\) that holds uniformly over stable matchings, and prove the corresponding statement for the full sums \(\|X(\mu)\|_1\) and \(\|Y(\mu)\|_1\).
location_in_paper: Page 7, Section 3.1 (Discussion); reiterated page 18, Section 7.1 (after Proposition 7.3).
open_problem_quote:
While we will establish such an approximation, we believe that, with a refined analysis, one should be able to show \(\sup_{\mu\in S}\,\bigl|n^{-1}\|X(\mu)\|_1\|Y(\mu)\|_1-1\bigr|\xrightarrow{p}0\).

We believe, however, that a refined analysis should suggest that \(\sup_{\mu\in S}\,\bigl|n^{-1}\|X(\mu)\|_1\|Y(\mu)\|_1-1\bigr|\xrightarrow{p}0\). This is the analogue of the "law of hyperbola" in Pittel (1992).
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement is a high-probability universal convergence claim over all stable matchings under a boundedness/contiguity condition; any violation would likely occur in rare-tail events rather than a small finite instance family. Searching finite n instances to disprove asymptotic convergence is not a reliable proxy, and computing the worst-case over all stable matchings is combinatorially hard.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: A coding agent could explore simulated markets, enumerate stable matchings for moderate n, and empirically study \(\max_{\mu\in S}|n^{-1}\|X(\mu)\|_1\|Y(\mu)\|_1-1|\) as n grows to guess the right normalization or identify problematic tail mechanisms. However, the key barrier is controlling heavy-tail contributions analytically; empirical fitness signals may suggest conjectures but are only an indirect proxy for a proof.

verification.notice: Extracted from the source paper and passed an automated fidelity audit; not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis: No relevant Scholar-organic forward citations were available for this problem. The forward-citation review therefore cannot identify follow-up work that resolves or materially advances the problem.
